subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The entire chapter is built on exploring and applying ratios — a specific form of fractions — in daily-life situations. Sections 7.1–7.6 develop the concept from image similarity through ratio simplification, proportionality checks, the Rule of Three (cross multiplication), partitioning quantities in a given ratio, and unit-conversion problems; all 12 worked examples and 26 exercises operationalise C-1.6 across diverse real-world contexts (lemonade, cement, profit sharing, tea price, bus capacity, farming, ancient Lilavati problems).

adjunct_competencies.justification: Section 7.4 derives the cross-multiplication rule algebraically (a:b::c:d → ad = bc → d = bc/a), and WE-9, WE-10, and WE-12 each set up and solve a linear equation in one unknown to find the missing fourth term in a proportion — substantively engaging the skill of posing and solving equations to find an unknown.

Section 7.4 explicitly introduces Aryabhata's Trairasika (Rule of Three, 499 CE) by name and explains his pramana:phala::ichchha:ichchhaphala formulation, which students use to solve WE-8 and WE-9; E-19 poses an original Lilavati problem attributing it to Bhaskara — both instances require students to recognise and apply contributions of named Indian mathematicians, not merely read about them.

Section 7.1 asks students to reason inductively from image measurements to discover why multiplicative change preserves similarity while additive change does not; Section 7.4 provides a deductive algebraic derivation of cross multiplication from the definition of proportionality; and the recurring Math Talk prompts throughout the chapter require students to formulate and evaluate arguments — collectively constituting substantive engagement with inductive and deductive logic.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to recognise, set up, and solve proportional relationships involving ratios by developing multiplicative reasoning — moving from visual similarity through ratio simplification and cross-multiplication to partitioning quantities — and applying these skills fluently across diverse real-world and historical contexts.
